rationales.hedonism: She "stayed past six" and is willing to add "one more review session" rather than protect her own comfort or rest.
rationales.achievement: She is intensely focused on outcomes, citing the "mid-term analysis spreadsheets" and celebrating students reading "a full grade level above where they started."
rationales.benevolence: She takes students' struggles personally, saying if "Ravi in 8B fails" it "sits in my chest," showing close, hands-on care for specific kids.
rationales.universalism: She frames the stakes in systemic terms, worrying students will "end up in the same cycle their parents were stuck in," which shows concern beyond individual performance.

rationales.hedonism: She emphasizes sacrifice over enjoyment, noting she "came in on Saturdays" and cannot take satisfaction in an above-average result.
rationales.achievement: She wants to be "First. Or at least second" and measures herself against other sections despite only three years on the job.

rationales.hedonism: She worked on supplementary exercises "instead of eating dinner," again subordinating immediate comfort to work.
rationales.achievement: She remains preoccupied with "the rankings" and spends extra time analyzing where students "lost marks" to improve performance.
rationales.universalism: She criticizes the exam for measuring "privilege" and notes students come from homes without books or instruction in the test's language, showing concern about structural unfairness.

rationales.benevolence: She is distressed that "Lakshmi in 7A has been complaining about stomach aches" and cannot stop thinking about the students drinking unsafe water.
rationales.universalism: She is collecting "six months of readings from twelve sites" and is willing to go "to the press" over contaminated water affecting children and the broader community.

rationales.hedonism: Even on Sunday she says she "told myself I'd take the day off but here I am at the kitchen table with three stacks of papers," denying herself rest.
rationales.achievement: She is juggling lesson prep, an NGO report, and a school self-evaluation while still rating her own "effort" as a five.
rationales.tradition: She vividly misses "Thrissur since Onam," her grandmother's "rice kanji," and the sensory familiarity of home, showing strong attachment to family heritage.
rationales.benevolence: She keeps postponing family connection, admitting she has not gone home in "Seven months" and the missing feels like something she "can't afford right now."

rationales.self_direction: She makes an autonomous limit-setting choice, telling the coordinator she can do fieldwork only "every other Saturday" despite feeling guilty.
rationales.universalism: She rejects the idea of letting "the NGO stuff go" because otherwise "the block office gets to keep ignoring it," showing continued commitment to a public-health issue.

rationales.self_direction: She independently rewrote the passages with "market scenes, monsoon flooding, cricket matches" instead of accepting the standard materials as given.
rationales.hedonism: She "slept maybe four hours" and worked until "2 AM," clearly sacrificing comfort and rest.
rationales.achievement: She painstakingly reworked the module late into the night and tracked that "the difference was immediate" when students tried it.
rationales.universalism: She is trying to bridge inequity in a test that assumes "a particular kind of childhood," adapting materials so excluded students can access them.

rationales.achievement: She documents measurable gains, noting the pass rate rose from "38% to 61%" and that she recorded the evidence carefully.
rationales.universalism: She pursues the water case and argues the standardized test is biased, insisting on "evidence, not just frustration" to challenge unfair systems.

rationales.achievement: She refuses to take "three days off" because she "need[s] to prep the students for boards," prioritizing work demands over personal life.
rationales.tradition: The tension centers on missing "Pongal," being driven to the library by her mother, and the family meaning wrapped into becoming a teacher.

rationales.hedonism: She reports that she "ate dinner at my desk every night this week" and accepts that the work does not make the tiredness go away.
rationales.achievement: She highlights concrete wins: district recognition, improved students, and submitting an "Emerging Educator portfolio" with evidence.
rationales.universalism: She explicitly says "The water report matters more than my comfort" and "My students' futures matter more than my sleep schedule," prioritizing broader welfare over self.

rationales.security: He immediately calculates the impact of the pay change, writes down the numbers, and worries he'd need "four extra hours a day" to preserve income.
He abandons the stable routine he trusts, admitting "I already have a routine that works" but broke it "trying to grab a little more," which created unnecessary financial and operational risk.
He restores the stable setup, saying he deleted the third app, went "Back to the two I know," and is already budgeting for brake pads because "the bike needs new brake pads soon."
He knowingly keeps riding with dangerous brakes, saying "every day I ride with bad brakes is a day something could go wrong" but still chooses "one more day" for the bonus.
He finally repairs the bike and emphasizes the restored safety and stability: "it's done and the bike stops clean now. Feel better on the road."
He refuses a risky investment because "Two million is two months of savings" and insists "the money stays where it is" rather than jeopardize the buffer he built.
The entry centers on instability he cannot control: missing deliveries mean "maybe sixty mil gone," and he hates relying on a system that leaves his income uncertain.
He took a shortcut he "didn't need to take" and turned a normally safe routine into a threat to his rating, queue priority, and ability to "eat next month."
He moves the bonus to savings "immediately," tracks the month in his notebook, and plans to cover the bike insurance "without dipping into savings."
He pays the bike insurance early, notes the savings account is at "the highest it's been since December," and is budgeting the plumbing repair in a careful "One thing at a time" sequence.
